subject: Chemistry
q: Two Faraday of electricity is passed through a solution of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> . The mass of copper deposited at the cathode is: (Atomic mass of Cu = 63.5 amu)
question: Two Faraday of electricity is passed through a solution of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> . The mass of copper deposited at the cathode is: (Atomic mass of Cu = 63.5 amu)
solution:
Cu+2 + 2e- → Cu

2 Faraday charge deposits 1 mol of Cu, or

2 F≡2 Equivalents of Cu

Mass of Cu deposited=2×63.52=63.5g

subject: Chemistry
q:
The potential for the given half cell at 298K is -............ ×10-2V.

2H(aq)++2e-→H2( g)

H+=1M,PH2=2 atm

(Given2.303 RT/F=0.06 V, log2=0.3)
question:
The potential for the given half cell at 298K is -............ ×10-2V.

2H(aq)++2e-→H2( g)

H+=1M,PH2=2 atm

(Given2.303 RT/F=0.06 V, log2=0.3)
solution:
The Nernst equation for the given cell reaction 2H++2e-→H2 is 

E=E°-0.062×log pH2H+2

E=0-0.062log212

E=-0.03×0.3=-0.009=-9×10-3

E=-0.9×10-2V

subject: Chemistry
q: The cell, $\mathrm{Zn}\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(1 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^0=1.10 \mathrm{~V}\right)$, was allowed to be completely discharged at $\mathrm{298~K}$. The relative concentration of $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\left[\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}\right]$ is
question: The cell, $\mathrm{Zn}\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(1 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^0=1.10 \mathrm{~V}\right)$, was allowed to be completely discharged at $\mathrm{298~K}$. The relative concentration of $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\left[\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}\right]$ is
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \mathrm{Q} \\
& \text { Where } \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}
\end{aligned}$
For complete discharge $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=0$
So $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\frac{0.591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}$
$\Rightarrow\left|\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}\right|=10^{37.3}$
Hence, (C) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following electrochemical cell at 298 K
$\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{HSnO}_{2}{ }^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}{ }^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})\left|\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \mid \mathrm{Bi}(\mathrm{s})$.
If the reaction quotient at a given time is $10^{6}$, then the cell EMF ($\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}$) is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~V}$ (Nearest integer).
Given the standard half-cell reduction potential as
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} / \mathrm{Bi}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}}^{\circ}=-0.44 \mathrm{~V} \text { and } \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}^{2-} / \mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}}^{\circ}=-0.90 \mathrm{~V}$
question:
Consider the following electrochemical cell at 298 K
$\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{HSnO}_{2}{ }^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}{ }^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})\left|\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s}) \mid \mathrm{Bi}(\mathrm{s})$.
If the reaction quotient at a given time is $10^{6}$, then the cell EMF ($\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}$) is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~V}$ (Nearest integer).
Given the standard half-cell reduction potential as
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} / \mathrm{Bi}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}}^{\circ}=-0.44 \mathrm{~V} \text { and } \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}^{2-} / \mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}}^{\circ}=-0.90 \mathrm{~V}$
solution:
The electrochemical cell has cathode:
$Bi_2O_3 + 3H_2O + 6e^- \rightarrow 2Bi + 6OH^-$ with $E° = -0.44$ V
and anode:
$Sn(OH)_6^{2-} - 2e^- \rightarrow HSnO_2^- + H_2O + OH^-$ with $E° = -0.90$ V.
The standard cell EMF is $E°_{cell} = -0.44 - (-0.90) = 0.46$ V.
Using the Nernst equation with $Q = 10^6$ and $n = 6$ electrons transferred:
$E_{cell} = 0.46 - \frac{0.059}{6} \log(10^6) = 0.46 - \frac{0.059 \times 6}{6} = 0.46 - 0.059 = 0.401$ V.
Expressing as $x \times 10^{-1}$ V:
$0.401 = 4.01 \times 10^{-1}$ V, so $x = 4$ (nearest integer).

At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, permanganate is expected to oxidize: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>0.059</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the standard reduction potentials are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>51</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> for <math><msubsup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>|</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> for <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>|</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> for <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>|</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>54</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> for <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>|</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, permanganate is expected to oxidize: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>0.059</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
MnO4-+8H++5e-→Mn2++4H2O 
EMnO4-Mn2+=Eo-0.0595log⁡Mn2+MnO4-H+8
=1.51-0.0595log⁡110-38
(Assuming MnO4-=Mn2+=1M )
=1.51-0.0595×24=1.51-0.28
=1.23 V
EredMnO4-Mn2+o=1.23 V>EredBr2Br-o>EredI2I-o 
i.e. it will oxidise Br- and I-, only.

The resistance of 0.5 M solution of the same electrolyte is <math> <mrow> <mn>28</mn> <mn>0</mn> <mi> Ω</mi> </mrow> </math>. The molar conductivity of 0.5 M solution of the electrolyte in <math> <mrow> <msup> <mtext>S m</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <msup> <mo>mol</mo> <mn>-1</mn> </msup> </mrow> </math> is :

Λ m = K C = 1 4 × 1 0.5 × 1 0 3 = 5 × 1 0 - 4  Ω -1 m 2 mol -1

subject: Chemistry
q:
To find the standard potential of M3+/M electrode, the following cell is constituted: Pt/M/M3+ 0.001 mol L-1/Ag+ 0.01 mol L-1/Ag

The emf of the cell is found to be 0.421 volt at 298 K. The standard potential of half-reaction M3++3e-→M at 298 K will be:

(Given: EAg+Ag⊝ at 298 K=0.80 volt)
question:
To find the standard potential of M3+/M electrode, the following cell is constituted: Pt/M/M3+ 0.001 mol L-1/Ag+ 0.01 mol L-1/Ag

The emf of the cell is found to be 0.421 volt at 298 K. The standard potential of half-reaction M3++3e-→M at 298 K will be:

(Given: EAg+Ag⊝ at 298 K=0.80 volt)
solution:
Ecell=Eocell−0.0591nlogQ

∴ Q=M3+Ag+3 =0.001(0.01)3=103

Ecell=Eocell−0.05913log103

Ecell=Eocell−0.05913×3=0.421Eocell−0.0591=0.421Eocell=0.48 V =EoAg+/Ag-EoM3+/MEoM3+/M=0.80-0.48 = 0.32 V

question:
Consider the following reaction

MnO4-+8H++5e-→Mn+2+4H2O ,E°=1.51 V

The quantity of electricity required in Faraday to reduce five moles of MnO4- is
solution:
For this balanced reaction, the change in oxidation number of the metal ion is Mn+7 →Mn+2.

Therefore one mole Mn required 5 Faraday

Hence, for five mole Mn required 25 Faraday.

& \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{~s}) / \mathrm{I}^{-}=+0.54 \mathrm{~V}, \\

Based on the above potentials, strongest oxidizing agent will be :
question:
Electrode potentials $\left(\mathrm{E}^{\circ}\right)$ are given below :
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Cu}^{+} / \mathrm{Cu}=+0.52 \mathrm{~V} ,\\
& \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=+0.77 \mathrm{~V}, \\
& \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{~s}) / \mathrm{I}^{-}=+0.54 \mathrm{~V}, \\
& \mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}=+0.88 \mathrm{~V}.
\end{aligned}$
Based on the above potentials, strongest oxidizing agent will be :
solution: Higher the value of reduction potential stronger will be the oxidising hence based on the given values $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$will be strongest oxidizing agent.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The specific conductance of 0.0025M acetic acid is 5×10-5 S cm-1 at a certain temperature. The dissociation constant of acetic acid is ___________ ×10-7.(Nearest integer)

Consider limiting molar conductivity of CH3COOH as 400 S cm2 mol-1
question:
The specific conductance of 0.0025M acetic acid is 5×10-5 S cm-1 at a certain temperature. The dissociation constant of acetic acid is ___________ ×10-7.(Nearest integer)

Consider limiting molar conductivity of CH3COOH as 400 S cm2 mol-1
solution:
The relation between Molar conductance λm and specific conductance κ is 

λm=k×1000M

M = molarity

λm=5×10-5×1032.5×10-3=20  S cm2 mol-1

Now, the degree of dissociation, α=λmλ∞

α=20400=120

The acid dissociation constant, Ka=Cα2(1-α)=2.5×10-3120×1201920

= 65.789 × 10–7

≈ 66 × 10–7

subject: Chemistry
q:
A battery is constructed of $\mathrm{Cr}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$. The unbalanced chemical equation when such a battery discharges is following:
$$
\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+\mathrm{Cr}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Na}^{+}
$$
If one Faraday of electricity is passed through the battery during the charging, the number of moles of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ removed from the solution is
question:
A battery is constructed of $\mathrm{Cr}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$. The unbalanced chemical equation when such a battery discharges is following:
$$
\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+\mathrm{Cr}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Na}^{+}
$$
If one Faraday of electricity is passed through the battery during the charging, the number of moles of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ removed from the solution is
solution:
Reduction half reaction :
$$
\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+6 e^{-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$
Oxidation half reaction :
$$
\mathrm{Cr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 e^{-}
$$
Overall reaction :
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+\mathrm{Cr}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+}+3 e^{-} \\
& 3 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
$3 \mathrm{~F}$ of electricity $=3$ moles of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$
$1 \mathrm{~F}$ of electricity $=\frac{3}{3}$ moles of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The magnitude of the change in oxidising power of the MnO4-/Mn2+ couple is x×10-4 V if the H+ concentration is decreased from 1 M to 10-4M at 25°C. (Assume concentration of MnO4- and Mn2+ to be same on change in H+ concentration). The value of x is __________.

(Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given: 2.303RTF=0.059]
question:
The magnitude of the change in oxidising power of the MnO4-/Mn2+ couple is x×10-4 V if the H+ concentration is decreased from 1 M to 10-4M at 25°C. (Assume concentration of MnO4- and Mn2+ to be same on change in H+ concentration). The value of x is __________.

(Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given: 2.303RTF=0.059]
solution:
Eqn is-

MnO4-+H⊕+5e-→Mn+2+4H2O

Nernst equation:

Ecell =ECell 0-0.0595logMn+2MnO4-1H+8

(I) Given H⊕=1M

E1=E0-0.0595logMn+2MnO4-

(II) Now: H⊕=10-4M

E2=E0-0.0595logMn+2MnO4-×110-48

=E0-0.0595logMn+2MnO4-+0.0595log10-32

therefore: E1-E2=0.0595×32

=0.3776 V=3776×10-4

x=3776

subject: Chemistry
q:
For a cell, CusCu2+0.001M)Ag+0.01MAgsthe cell potential is found to be 0.43 V at 298 K. The magnitude of standard electrode potential for Cu2+|Cu is____×10-2 V

Given: EAg+/AgΘ=0.80 V and 2.303RTF=0.06 V
question:
For a cell, CusCu2+0.001M)Ag+0.01MAgsthe cell potential is found to be 0.43 V at 298 K. The magnitude of standard electrode potential for Cu2+|Cu is____×10-2 V

Given: EAg+/AgΘ=0.80 V and 2.303RTF=0.06 V
solution:
At anode Cu→Cu2++2e-

At cathode 2Ag++2e-→2Ag

Cell reaction →Cu+2Ag+→Cu2++2Ag

Ecell=Ecell0-0.062logCu2+Ag+2

0.43=Ecell0-0.062log0.0010.012

Ecell0=0.46

Ecell0=EAg+/Ag0-ECu2+/Cu0

0.46=0.80-ECu2+/Cu0

ECu2+/Cu0=0.34 volt

ECu2+/Cu0=34×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q: For a \(\mathrm{Mg}\left|\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})\right|\left|\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Ag}\) the correct Nernst Equation is :
question: For a \(\mathrm{Mg}\left|\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})\right|\left|\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Ag}\) the correct Nernst Equation is :
solution:
$\begin{array}{lll}\text { Cathode } & \left(\mathrm{Ag}+(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{e}^{-}\right. & \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}) \times 2 \\ \text { Anode } & \mathrm{Mg} & \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}\end{array}$
By Nernst equation $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \mathrm{Q} \\ & \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \frac{\left[\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^2} \\ & =\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \frac{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\right]} \end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Calculate the standard cell potential (in V) of the cell in which the following reaction takes place:

Fe2+ aq+Ag+aq→Fe3+aq+Ag(s)

Given that
EAg+/Ago=x V
EFe2+/Feo=y V
EFe3+/Feo=z V
question:
Calculate the standard cell potential (in V) of the cell in which the following reaction takes place:

Fe2+ aq+Ag+aq→Fe3+aq+Ag(s)

Given that
EAg+/Ago=x V
EFe2+/Feo=y V
EFe3+/Feo=z V
solution:
Fe+2A+Ag+C→Fe3++Ag

Ecello=SRP of cathode - SRP of anode
=EAg+|Ago-EFe+3|Fe+2o
=x-EFe3+|Fe+2o

Since E° cell is an intensive property, the E° cell values are not additive by nature. But the standard Gibbs free energy change is an extensive property and hence can be added.

(i) Fe+3→Fe+3e-→Z=E1o;  ΔG1=-3×F×z

(ii) Fe+2→Fe+2e- Y=E2o;  ΔG2=-2×F×y

Subtracting (ii) from (i)
Fe+3→Fe+2 ΔG3=ΔG1-ΔG2

Eo=-2y+3z

Ecello=x--2y+3z

=x+2y-3z

The metal ions which can liberate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> from a dilute acid are
question: The standard electrode potential (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>) for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Cr</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Mn</mi></math> & <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Co</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>41</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>57</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>97</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, respectively. The metal ions which can liberate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> from a dilute acid are
solution:
The E⊝ values for the redox couple (M3+/M2+) shows that Mn2+ and Co2+ ions are the strongest oxidising agents in aqueous solution.

The ions Ti2+, V2+ and Cr2+ are strong reducing agents and will liberate hydrogen from a dilute acid.

So, the Correct answer is V2+ and Cr2+.

After a period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> minutes, the increase in mass of cathode was <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>314</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>. The total charge passed through the solution is ___<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>. (Given atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Au</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>197</mn><mo>)</mo></math>
question: A constant current was passed through a solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>AuCl</mi><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></msubsup></math> ion between gold electrodes. After a period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> minutes, the increase in mass of cathode was <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>314</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>. The total charge passed through the solution is ___<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>. WE= charge1 F

1.3141973=Q1F

subject: Chemistry
q:
For a general redox reaction
Anode : $\text{Red}_1 \rightarrow \text{Ox}_1^{n_1^+} + n_1 e^-$
Cathode : $\text{Ox}_2 + n_2 e^- \rightarrow \text{Red}_2^{n_2^-}$
Which of the following statement is incorrect ?
question:
For a general redox reaction
Anode : $\text{Red}_1 \rightarrow \text{Ox}_1^{n_1^+} + n_1 e^-$
Cathode : $\text{Ox}_2 + n_2 e^- \rightarrow \text{Red}_2^{n_2^-}$
Which of the following statement is incorrect ?
options: The overall reaction can be written as $n_2\text{Red}_1 + n_1\text{Ox}_2 \rightleftharpoons n_2\text{Ox}_1^{n_1^+} + n_1\text{Red}_2^{n_2^-}$
The electrons do not appear in the overall reaction because electrons produced at the anode are consumed at the cathode.
Here $n$ is the number of electrons transferred in redox reaction.
If the reaction is carried out reversibly, the electrical work done is equal to the ratio of charge and potential difference through which charge is moved.
solution:
For the given redox reaction, the half-cell reactions are:
Anode: $\text{Red}_1 \rightarrow \text{Ox}_1^{n_1^+} + n_1 e^-$
Cathode: $\text{Ox}_2 + n_2 e^- \rightarrow \text{Red}_2^{n_2^-}$

To obtain the overall balanced chemical equation, we multiply the anode reaction by $n_2$ and the cathode reaction by $n_1$ so that the number of electrons lost equals the number of electrons gained:
$n_2\text{Red}_1 \rightarrow n_2\text{Ox}_1^{n_1^+} + n_1 n_2 e^-$
$n_1\text{Ox}_2 + n_1 n_2 e^- \rightarrow n_1\text{Red}_2^{n_2^-}$

Adding these gives the overall reaction:
$n_2\text{Red}_1 + n_1\text{Ox}_2 \rightleftharpoons n_2\text{Ox}_1^{n_1^+} + n_1\text{Red}_2^{n_2^-}$
Thus, statement (A) is correct.

In the overall reaction, electrons do not appear because the electrons produced at the anode are exactly consumed at the cathode. Thus, statement (B) is correct.

From the Nernst equation:
$E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q = E^\circ - \dfrac{2.303 RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$
$\dfrac{E - E^\circ}{RT/F} = -\dfrac{2.303}{n} \log_{10} Q$
The graph of $\dfrac{E - E^\circ}{RT/F}$ versus $\log_{10} Q$ is a straight line with a slope of $-\dfrac{2.303}{n}$, where $n$ is the number of electrons transferred in the balanced redox reaction. Thus, statement (C) is correct.

The electrical work done by a galvanic cell is given by the product of the total charge transferred and the cell potential (potential difference), not their ratio.
$W_{\text{electrical}} = \text{Charge} \times \text{Potential difference} = nF E_{\text{cell}}$
Thus, statement (D) is incorrect.

Answer: If the reaction is carried out reversibly, the electrical work done is equal to the ratio of charge and potential difference through which charge is moved.

subject: Chemistry
q: The metal that cannot be obtained by the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of its salt is
question: The metal that cannot be obtained by the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of its salt is
solution:
For the reduction of H2O at cathode,

2H2O+2e-→H2g+2OH-; Ered∘=-1.23V

For Ca2++2e-→Ca; Ered∘=-2.87V

i.e., water will be reduced at the cathode, in preference to the liberation of Ca.

However, in the case of Ag, Cu and Cr, their standard reduction potentials are higher than -1.23 V and will be liberated/ deposited at the cathode and H2O will not be reduced.

solution:
H2O→12O2+2H++2e-

Electricity required to oxidise 1mol H2O to O2=2F

WE=Q96500

mole ×n-factor =Q96500

1×2=Q96500

Q=2×96500C

=1.93×105C

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : The E° value of Ce4+/Ce3+ is +1.74 V

Statement II : Ce is more stable in Ce4+ state than Ce3+ state.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : The E° value of Ce4+/Ce3+ is +1.74 V

Statement II : Ce is more stable in Ce4+ state than Ce3+ state.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both statement I and statement II are correct
Statement I is incorrect but statement II is correct
Both statement I and statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
solution:
The E° value for Ce4+/Ce3+ is +1.74 V because the most stable oxidation state of lanthanide series elements is +3.

It means Ce3+ is more stable than Ce4+.

subject: Chemistry
q: When during electrolysis of a solution of $\mathrm{AgNO}_3 9650$ coulombs of charge pass through the electroplating bath, the mass of silver deposited on the cathode will be
question: When during electrolysis of a solution of $\mathrm{AgNO}_3 9650$ coulombs of charge pass through the electroplating bath, the mass of silver deposited on the cathode will be
solution:
No. of moles of silver $=\frac{9650}{96500}=\frac{1}{10}$ moles
$\therefore$ Mass of silver deposited $=\frac{1}{10} \times 108=10.8 \mathrm{~g}$

subject: Chemistry
q: How many electrons would be required to deposit $6.35 \mathrm{~g}$ of copper at the cathode during the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of copper sulphate? (Atomic mass of copper $=63.5 \mathrm{u}, \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=$ Avogadro's constant):
question: How many electrons would be required to deposit $6.35 \mathrm{~g}$ of copper at the cathode during the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of copper sulphate? (Atomic mass of copper $=63.5 \mathrm{u}, \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=$ Avogadro's constant):
solution:
$\mathrm{Cu} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{++}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
i.e, to deposit 1 mole of $\mathrm{Cu}$ at cathode from $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ solution $=2$ moles of electrons are required
i.e, To deposit
$6.35 \mathrm{~g}=\frac{6.35}{63.5} \times 2=\frac{2}{10}=\frac{1}{5}$ moles
Thus total no. of electrons required $=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}}{5}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following statements is correct for electrolysis of brine solution?
question: Which one of the following statements is correct for electrolysis of brine solution?
solution:
Brine solution is the concentrated solution of NaCl in water.

 NaCl (aq.)→Na(aq) ++Cl(aq) +

The cathode will attract the sodium ions from the sodium chloride and hydrogen ions from the water. The hydrogen ions gain 2 electrons and are reduced to form hydrogen molecules.

2H+ (aq) + 2e- → H2 (g)

2H2O + 2e− → H2 (g) + 2OH− (aq)

The anode will attract the hydroxide ions from the water and chlorine ions from sodium chloride. The chlorine ions lose 2 electrons and get oxidised.

Cl2 → 2Cl− + 2e−

Na+ + OH− → NaOH

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following reduction processes:

Zn2++2e-→Zn(s);Eo=-0.76 V

Ca2++2e-→Ca(s);Eo=-2.87 V

Mg2++2e-→Mg(s);Eo=-2.36 V

Ni2++2e-→Ni(s);Eo=-0.25 V

The reducing power of the metals increases in the order:
question:
Consider the following reduction processes:

Zn2++2e-→Zn(s);Eo=-0.76 V

Ca2++2e-→Ca(s);Eo=-2.87 V

Mg2++2e-→Mg(s);Eo=-2.36 V

Ni2++2e-→Ni(s);Eo=-0.25 V

The reducing power of the metals increases in the order:
solution:
Lower the standard reduction potential more will be the reducing power i.e gets self oxidised.

Therefore the correct order is:

Ni<Zn<Mg<Ca

subject: Chemistry
q: One Faraday of electricity liberates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> gram atom of copper from copper sulphate, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is______.
question: One Faraday of electricity liberates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> gram atom of copper from copper sulphate, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is______.
solution:
One Faraday of electricity means it is equivalent to one mole of electrons charge.

Cu2++2e–→Cu

2 Faraday →1 mol Cu

1 Faraday →0.5 mol Cu deposit

0.5 mol=0.5 g atom=5×10–1

x=5

question: The reduction potential of hydrogen half cell will be negative if :
solution:
$2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}^{\circ}-0.059 \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^2}\right)$ (here $\mathrm{E}$ is -ve when $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_2}>\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^2$ )
$=\frac{-0.0591}{2} \log _{10}\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)=\frac{-.0591}{2} \times .3010=$ negative value

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : The limiting molar conductivity of KCl (strong electrolyte) is higher compared to that of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ (weak electrolyte).
Statement II : Molar conductivity decreases with decrease in concentration of electrolyte.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : The limiting molar conductivity of KCl (strong electrolyte) is higher compared to that of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ (weak electrolyte).
Statement II : Molar conductivity decreases with decrease in concentration of electrolyte.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
lon K+ Cl- CH3COO- H+
λ∘/ S cm2 mol-1 73.5 76.3 40.9 349.6

λ∘MCH3COOH=λ∘MH++λ∘MCH3COO−

=349.6+40.9=390.5Scm2mol−1

λ∘M(KCl)=λ∘MK++λ∘MCl−

=73.5+76.3=149.8Scm2mol−1

So, statement-I is false.
For an electrolyte the dilution increases (or) decrease in concentration, the number of ions increases due to increase of degree of dissociation. So, the molar conductance increases. Hence, Statement-II is false.

The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
question: The solubility product of a sparingly soluble salt <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If specific conductance of the solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>Sm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the limiting molar conductivity of the solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
solution:
KspA2X3=1.1×10-23=110×10-25

Ksp=2233s5=110×10-25

4×27s5=110×10-25;  S=M=1×10-5

K=3×10-5Sm-1
